questions.stem: By Proposition 3.2, a finite subgroup of $\mathrm{Isom}(E^2)$ must be which kind of group?
options: Either a cyclic group of rotations or a dihedral group of rotations and reflections.
Either a translation group or a glide reflection group.
questions.explanation: A finite plane isometry group fixes a centroid $c$. It is either cyclic, consisting of $N$ rotations about $c$, or dihedral, consisting of $N$ rotations and $N$ reflections in lines through $c$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Why must every finite subgroup $G$ of $\mathrm{Isom}(E^N)$ fix some point?
options: Because every isometry has a fixed point.
Because the centroid $c = \frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{T \in G} T(a)$ of any orbit is fixed by all of $G$.
Because translations always fix the origin.
questions.explanation: Isometries are affine, so they commute with the averaging that defines the centroid of an orbit. Hence every element of the finite group $G$ fixes that centroid $c$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The full symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon in the plane has how many elements?
questions.explanation: The symmetries are $N$ rotations and $N$ reflections, giving $2N$ in total. This is the dihedral group of the regular $N$-gon.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rotational (orientation preserving) symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon is which group?
questions.explanation: The orientation preserving symmetries are the $N$ rotations through angles $2\pi k/N$, forming a cyclic group of order $N$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the full symmetry group of a square?
questions.explanation: A square is a regular $4$-gon, so its symmetry group has $2 \times 4 = 8$ elements: $4$ rotations and $4$ reflections.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the full symmetry group of a regular hexagon?
questions.explanation: A regular hexagon is a regular $6$-gon, so its symmetry group has $2 \times 6 = 12$ elements: $6$ rotations and $6$ reflections.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many reflection symmetries does a regular pentagon have?
questions.explanation: A regular $N$-gon has $N$ reflection axes, so the pentagon has $5$ reflections (each through a vertex and the midpoint of the opposite edge). It also has $5$ rotations, giving $10$ symmetries in total.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the rotational symmetry group (orientation preserving symmetries) of a regular octagon?
questions.explanation: The orientation preserving symmetries of a regular $8$-gon are its $8$ rotations, forming a cyclic group of order $8$. The full group, including reflections, has order $16$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The homomorphism $\varepsilon : \mathrm{Isom}(E^N) \to \{-1, +1\}$ sends an isometry $A : x \mapsto Bx + v$ to which value?
questions.explanation: The orientation homomorphism is $\varepsilon(A) = \det B$. Its kernel is the index $2$ subgroup $\mathrm{Isom}^+(E^N)$ of orientation preserving isometries.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The kernel of the homomorphism $\varphi : \mathrm{Isom}(E^N) \to O(N)$, $A \mapsto B$ (the orthogonal part), is which subgroup?
questions.explanation: An isometry $x \mapsto Bx + v$ is in the kernel when $B = I$, that is when it is a translation. So $\ker \varphi = \mathrm{Trans}(E^N)$, a normal subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The subgroup $\mathrm{Isom}^+(E^N)$ of orientation preserving isometries has which index in $\mathrm{Isom}(E^N)$?
questions.explanation: Since $\varepsilon$ maps onto $\{-1, +1\}$, its kernel $\mathrm{Isom}^+(E^N)$ has index $2$. Its two cosets are the orientation preserving and orientation reversing isometries.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a subgroup $G$ of $\mathrm{Isom}(E^N)$, let $G^+$ be its orientation preserving elements. Which statement always holds?
questions.explanation: The restriction of $\varepsilon$ to $G$ has image $\{+1\}$ or $\{-1, +1\}$. So $G = G^+$ (index $1$) or $G^+$ is the kernel of a surjection onto $\{-1, +1\}$ and has index $2$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: In the dihedral symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon, if $R$ is the basic rotation and $M$ a reflection, the products $M, RM, R^2 M, \dots, R^{N-1}M$ are which isometries?
options: All reflections in lines through the centre.
questions.explanation: Each $R^k M$ is orientation reversing and fixes the centre, so it is a reflection. These are the $N$ distinct reflections of the dihedral group, in lines obtained by rotating the mirror.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A finite subgroup of $\mathrm{Isom}(E^2)$ consists only of orientation preserving isometries. What can it be?
options: A cyclic group of rotations about a common centre.
A group containing a glide reflection.
questions.explanation: If $G = G^+$ then all elements preserve orientation and fix the centroid, so they are rotations about a common point. A finite group of such rotations is cyclic.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An equilateral triangle has a symmetry group of which order, and which structure?
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle is a regular $3$-gon, so its symmetry group has $2 \times 3 = 6$ elements: $3$ rotations and $3$ reflections. This is the dihedral group of the triangle.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A figure in the plane has exactly $5$ rotational symmetries and no reflection symmetry. Its full symmetry group is which group?
questions.explanation: With $5$ rotations and no reflections, the group consists only of orientation preserving isometries, so it is the cyclic group of order $5$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For the dihedral symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon, the relation between a reflection $M$ and the basic rotation $R$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Conjugating a rotation by a reflection reverses the rotation: $M R M^{-1} = R^{-1}$. This is the defining relation of the dihedral group and shows it is non-abelian for $N \ge 3$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The symmetry group of a non-square rectangle (with unequal side lengths) has which order?
questions.explanation: A non-square rectangle has the identity, a half-turn, and two reflections (in the two axes through the midpoints of opposite sides), giving order $4$. It is not a regular polygon, so its group is smaller than the square's order $8$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The basic rotation generating the cyclic part of the symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon turns through which angle?
questions.explanation: The smallest nontrivial rotation symmetry of a regular $N$-gon is through $2\pi/N$. Its powers give all $N$ rotations through $2\pi k/N$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many rotational symmetries (including the identity) does a regular $12$-gon have?
questions.explanation: A regular $N$-gon has exactly $N$ rotational symmetries, counting the identity. For $N = 12$ that is $12$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Using the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem on the action of the dihedral symmetry group of a regular $N$-gon on its $N$ vertices, the stabilizer of a single vertex has which order?
questions.explanation: The full group has order $2N$ and the vertices form one orbit of size $N$, so each stabilizer has order $2N/N = 2$. The stabilizer of a vertex is the identity together with the reflection through that vertex.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which of these is the symmetry group of a pattern fixed by the infinite group generated by a single nonzero translation of $E^2$, with no other symmetries?
questions.explanation: A single translation generates an infinite cyclic group (isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}$). Such a group is not finite, so it falls outside Proposition 3.2, which classifies the finite plane isometry groups.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Give the order of the full symmetry group of a regular decagon (a regular $10$-gon), counting both rotations and reflections.
questions.solution: A regular $N$-gon has $2N$ symmetries. For $N = 10$ this is {{1}}.
questions.explanation: The decagon has $10$ rotations and $10$ reflections, for $2 \times 10 = 20$ symmetries in total.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A regular heptagon (a regular $7$-gon). Give the number of its reflection symmetries.
questions.solution: A regular $N$-gon has exactly $N$ reflection axes, so the heptagon has {{1}} reflections.
questions.explanation: A regular $7$-gon has $7$ reflection axes, each through one vertex and the midpoint of the opposite edge.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A planar figure has exactly $6$ rotational symmetries and no reflections. State whether its full symmetry group is cyclic.
questions.solution: With no orientation reversing symmetries, the group consists only of rotations, so it {{1}} cyclic.
questions.explanation: A finite group of orientation preserving plane isometries is cyclic. With $6$ rotations and no reflections, it is the cyclic group of order $6$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The dihedral symmetry group of a regular $9$-gon acts on its $9$ vertices, which form a single orbit. Using $|G| = 18$ and the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem, give the order of the stabilizer of one vertex.
questions.solution: The stabilizer order is $|G|/|\mathrm{Orb}| = 18/9$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: The group has order $2 \times 9 = 18$ and the vertices form one orbit of size $9$, so each stabilizer has order $18/9 = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
